---
title: "Introduction to Git"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ./diagrams/title-slide-background.svg
  data-background-size: 90%
  data-background-opacity: "0.5"
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: default
---

# Overview

- Part I - Basic Git

- Part II - Branching and remotes

- Part III - Advanced topics


# Part I - Basic Git


## Prerequisites

- Working on a Windows machine

- Has [Git for Windows](https://gitforwindows.org/){target="_blank"} installed

- Basic configuration of Git, ie set name/email/core.editor


## Part I - Introduction to Git

What is Git?

Configuring Git

Introduction to basic commands and use of Git


## What is Git?

Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS)

Software that tracks versions of files

Each version is a snapshot of the files at that point



::: {.notes}
Git is a **distributed** version control system (DVCS). A version control
system (VCS) is software that tracks versions of files. In contrast to
**centralized** VCS's, A DVCS does not  have a central code repository where files
are stoed. Instead every user has the entire history of versions of files.

Git stores versions of files as "snapshots" in contrasts to many other VCS's
where differences between versions of files are stored.
:::

## Why should you use Git to version control your code?

Manually storing old versions of code manually is haphazard and of limited use.
Using Git can help with:

- Switching back and forth between different versions of files

- Seeing exact differences between versions of code

- Facilitate working multiple people on the same code.

::: {.notes}

:::




## Configuration of Git

So how do we get started:

- Download Git for windows

- Configure Git Bash


::: {.notes}
We assume that the reader has already installed Git for Windows and has configured
the Git Bash (set user credentials and default editor). If not, see notes document for a guide.
:::


## Initialize a Git repository

Make a local directory into a Git repository:

1) Change directory to local directory using cd command

:::{.callout-note appearance="minimal" style="font-family: monospace"}
cd "S:/some path/probably/with spaces"
:::

2) Use git init command to initialize Git repository in folder

:::{.callout-note appearance="minimal" style="font-family: monospace"}
git init
:::


::: {.notes}

Note that it is only necessary to quote the filepath with the cd
command if the filepath contains spaces.

The file directory is shown as  "/s/some path/probably/with spaces"in Git Bash.
This form can also be used, ie "cd /s/some path/probably/with space".

It is often easier to right click (or shift+right click on Windows 11) on the folder
of interest and select "Open with Git Bash here".

Running git init in a directory creates a new subdirectory named ".git" that contains
all of your necessary repository files. We will not talk about what the content of ".git"
is at this point, but might come back to some of it later.

:::

## Running example I

Start a running example of live coding at this point?

::: {.notes}

1) Make a directory
2) Change directory in Git Bash to the newly created directory
2) Make a plain README text file in the directory. Do it using touch/echo commands
   to showcase using Git Bash.

:::

## Recording changes


## The lifecycle of the status of files

![](diagrams/lifecycle-status-files.svg){width=90% fig-align="center"}

::: {.aside}
Adaptation of diagram from Pro Git p. 26
:::


::: {.notes}

:::

# Part II - Branching and remotes

## Part II - Branching and remoteso

- Part II: Using git in day to day work
  - Simple ways of using Git in projects
  - Initialize a repository in an existing directory
  - Branching
  - Exercises meant to illustrate a simple but good workflow

::: {.notes}

:::


# Part III - Advanced topics

## Part III - Advanced topics

- Advanced commands. Like reset, rebase, diff etc
- Remotes
- GitHub
- Aliases
- Collaboration on a codebase
- Distributed workflows.
- Git GUI/IDE's. What are popular choices. Pro's and con's over using only Git Bash. 
- Version controlling output. Can it be justified in practice? Probably, but when and to
  what extent. What should you be extra cautious about when doing this? Large files and many commits?
- Recovering lost code. What do you do if you mess up and delete important files / commits.
- Git versus Subversion / distributed versus centralized VCS's.

::: {.notes}
Advanced/non-essential stand-alone topics that could be slides and/or included in the notes. 
Some of there topics will likely be incorporated into one of the earlier sections
:::
